macro_segments.title: Introduction to Nichbal: The Injured Party's Oath in Ambiguous Injury Cases

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Nichbal Case: Witnesses to Entry and Exit but Not to the Injury
Rav Yehuda's Teaching: The Shvua Must Be for Self-Infliction, Not Wall Contact
Rabbi Chiyya's Teaching: Injury Location Proves Human Agency, Eliminating Other Causes
Concern for Third Parties: The Requirement of Proof That Only Two Parties Were Present

macro_segments.title: When the Opposing Party Is Suspect: The Reversal of Oath Obligations

micro_segments.title: The Principle of Chashud Al Ha-Shvua: Even Shvuat Shav Disqualifies from Testifying Oaths
Distinguishing Shvuat Shav from Shvuat Bitui: Knowledge of Falsity at Moment of Oath
Rashi's Interpretation: Every Oath About the Past Qualifies as Shvuat Shav for Suspicion

macro_segments.title: The Disqualifying Categories: From Shvuat Shav to Gamblers and Violators of Torah Law

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's List of Disqualified Oath-Takers: Chashud Al Ha-Shvua and Halachic Violators
D'Oraisa vs. D'Rabbanan Disqualifications: Shvuat Shav Creates De'oraisa Psul; Gamblers Create D'rabbanan Psul

macro_segments.title: When Both Parties Are Suspect: The Machloket Between Rabbi Yossi and Rabbi Meir

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Dispute: Rabbi Yossi's Chazra Shvua Limkomah vs. Rabbi Meir's Yachloku
Rava's Question to Rav Nachman: Uncertainty About Which Opinion Is Correct
Confirming Rav Nachman's Position: Traditions Supporting Rabbi Yossi's Yachloku Opinion
Text Inversion Theory: Our Mishna May Have Flipped the Two Opinions

macro_segments.title: Interpreting Chazra Shvua Limkomah: The Babylonian vs. Eretz Yisrael Dispute

micro_segments.title: Rav and Shmuel's Position: Chazra Shvua L'Sinai—The Oath Disappears Entirely
Rabbi Abba's Position: Chazra Shvua L'Mechuyav Lah—The Oath Returns to the Originally Obligated Party

macro_segments.title: The Orphans' Loan Case: Demonstrating the Difference Between Babylonian and Eretz Yisrael Approaches

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Rule: Orphans Must Repay Loans Only with an Oath
Rav and Shmuel's Limitation: The Rule Applies Only When the Borrower Dies Before the Lender
The Inheritance Problem: Oaths Cannot Be Inherited from Father to Son
Rav and Shmuel's Conclusion: Without Inheritable Oath, the Debt Liability Disappears

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Abba's Case of the Stolen Metal: Proving the Alternative Interpretation

micro_segments.title: The Factual Setup: One Witness to Theft, Defendant Claims Original Ownership
Rabbi Ami's Dilemma: Can We Tell Him to Swear, Pay, or Go Free?
Rabbi Abba's Resolution: The Obligated Party Who Cannot Swear Must Pay
Rava's Agreement: Rabbi Abba's Approach Makes Better Sense Than Rav and Shmuel's

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Ami's Teaching and Its Limits: When the Psuk About Inheritors Applies

micro_segments.title: The Psuk's Application: Distinguishing Cases Where an Oath Would Be Obligatory
The Case of Uncertain Borrowing: When the Defendant Says 'I Don't Know' About Half the Debt
Why the Psuk Is Necessary: Proof That Oaths Cannot Be Inherited by Inheritors

macro_segments.title: Rav and Shmuel's Use of the Shvuat Hashem Psuk: An Alternative Derasha from Shimon ben Tarfon

micro_segments.title: Shimon ben Tarfon's Teaching: The Oath Lands on Both Parties—A Moral-Spiritual Interpretation
Additional Teachings of Shimon ben Tarfon: Various Derashot on Prohibition and Greatness

macro_segments.title: The Storekeeper's Ledger Case: Multiple Oaths Among Three Parties

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Scenario: Storekeeper Advances Wages, Then Disputes Payment to Workers
Rabbi's Objection: Why Enable a Shvua at All When Someone Must Be Lying?
Rabbi Chiya bar Abba's Answer: Both Parties Swear and Collect from the Householder
Rabbi's Opinion: Only Workers Swear to the Storekeeper
Rava's Opinion: Workers Swear to the Householder in the Storekeeper's Presence

macro_segments.title: Conflicting Witness Groups: Introduction to the Case of Shtei Kitei Eidim

micro_segments.title: Two Sets of Witnesses Contradicting Each Other: The Framework of the Problem
Rav Huna's Approach: Each Set Testifies Independently, Both Are Believed
Rav Chisda's Approach: The Case Remains in Limbo or Splits the Difference
